cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about what the films children watch at school actually do, according to the text. The options are A, B, C, D. First, I need to go back to the relevant part of the text. The passage mentions that children "view motion pictures which are supposedly meant to teach them how to deal with arms safely but which are actually designed to stimulate a desire to own a gun." So the key words here are that the films are supposed to teach safety but actually aim to stimulate desire for owning a gun. Looking at the options: A says they teach how to deal with guns safely. But the text says that's what they're "supposedly meant to" do, but the actual purpose is different. So A is not the correct answer because the question is about what they actually do, not what they claim to do. B says they praise hunting as character-building. The text does mention that gunners describe their actions as character-building, but the specific part about the films says they are designed to "stimulate a desire to own a gun." There's no direct mention of character-building in the context of the films, though the author later argues against the idea that hunting is character-building. So maybe B is not the answer here. C says they describe hunting as an exercise. The text doesn't mention exercise in relation to the films. The focus is on the desire to own a gun. D says they encourage them to have guns of their own.
